original_abstract: Transformers have demonstrated exceptional performance across natural language processing and computer vision, sparking significant interest in their application to time series analysis. Their inherent ability to model long-range dependencies and complex interactions makes them particularly well-suited for temporal data, resulting in a surge of specialized architectures. This paper provides a systematic and comprehensive review of Transformer variants designed for time series modeling. We evaluate these developments from two primary angles: network structure, focusing on adaptations made to address the unique challenges of temporal data (such as positional encoding and attention efficiency), and application domains, categorizing models by tasks like forecasting, anomaly detection, and classification. Furthermore, we conduct extensive empirical evaluations—including robustness tests, model size analysis, and seasonal-trend decomposition studies—to assess how these architectures truly perform in practical scenarios. By synthesizing current progress and identifying existing limitations, we propose key directions for future research to guide the continued evolution of Transformers in the time series community.
humanized_abstract: The remarkable performance of Transformers in natural language processing and computer vision in the recent years has motivated many researchers to apply them to time series analysis. Due to their unique abilities of learning long-term dependencies and complex patterns in data, many new Transformer-based architectures have been proposed, which motivates us to conduct a comprehensive survey on the recent advancements of Transformers for time series analysis from two aspects: 1) Network structure: We summarize the ways in which the basic structure of vanilla Transformers is modified to handle the characteristics of time series data, such as positional encoding and efficient attention, and 2) Application fields: Based on the tasks they are used for, we classify time series Transformers into forecasting, anomaly detection and classification, and discuss their applications and effectiveness. In addition, in order to validate the robustness of time series Transformers, we carry out a series of experiments which involves model size comparison, robustness evaluation of various designs, and validation of their ability to handle highly differing seasonal and trend components. Finally, our goal is to summarize the current researches and summarize the trends and future works of time series Transformers via our comprehensive survey.
